data.title: In 2023, Jeff Yan discussed high-frequency computing and why Hyperliquid was developed.
data.description:
This episode features Jeff Yan, founder of Hyperliquid. Jeff began his career in the high-frequency trading (HF) space at Hudson River before moving into the crypto world, where he built one of the largest market makers in the sector. He delves into the infrastructure of centralized crypto exchanges, adversarial algorithms, and why HFT (high-frequency trading) profits and losses can actually be predictive of medium-term price movements. He explains what he sees as the problems with current decentralized exchanges and introduces the early ideas behind Hyperliquid. This episode, released on May 8, 2023, offers a glimpse into many of Jeff Yan's early thoughts. How to Get into Crypto Trading from Harvard

Jeff Yan: My experience is similar to many HFT practitioners: I graduated from Harvard University with a major in Computer Science and Mathematics, and then directly joined Hudson River Trading, a large market-making institution in traditional finance. I traded US stocks at the time, and the experience was excellent. When I joined, the company had about 150 people, and now it's much larger. I benefited greatly from this experience, being exposed to the most interesting problems. Engineering and mathematics can be perfectly combined, which is almost "heaven" for quantitative trading. In 2018, with the rise of Ethereum smart contracts, I read the (Ethereum) Yellow Paper and instantly "got it." I was convinced that this was the future, so I left to work on an L2-oriented exchange protocol. We chose to enter the prediction market sector because Augur was already showing strong product-market fit (PMF), and we were more skilled in and focused on the underlying technical capabilities of exchanges. Therefore, after securing funding, we moved to San Francisco to build our team. However, a few months later, I decided to shut down the project because the timing wasn't right: regulatory uncertainty was extremely high, and user acquisition was very difficult. At that time, most people weren't familiar with smart contracts and were more interested in token speculation; the real demand for DeFi hadn't yet materialized, so the project was ultimately shelved. I then spent some time reflecting and traveling before finally returning to trading. Compared to constantly searching for PMF in the market, trading itself was more direct and interesting. Initially, I considered joining an established company, but considering my experience with crypto products and my familiarity with industry mechanisms, I started with proprietary crypto trading. Initially, this was just a side business, but I quickly saw significant opportunities, and the business expanded much faster than expected. The market's inefficiency surprised me. I then dedicated myself to it for nearly three years: a truly systematic launch occurred in early 2020, coinciding perfectly with the market cycle. As the market size and trading volume grew tenfold or even a hundredfold, we grew in tandem, eventually reaching the top tier of centralized exchange (CEX) market makers. About a year ago, we began systematically evaluating DeFi trading opportunities. This was similar to our early observations when entering CEX trading—inefficiencies were widespread. However, the difference was that some DeFi protocols had inherent flaws in their mechanism design, limiting the trading experience and capital efficiency. Simultaneously, after the FTX incident, the market's awareness of "not your keys, not your coins" and counterparty risk significantly strengthened, leading to a continued rise in demand for truly decentralized products. Based on the above changes, we believe the window of opportunity for building decentralized exchanges has arrived. Over the past one to two quarters, we have continuously invested resources in advancing this direction; our high-frequency trading (HFT) business is operating and maintaining relatively stably, while our current main investment and focus is on solidly implementing and systematically building this decentralized trading technology stack. Market Making vs. Order Taking: What's the Difference? Jeff Yan: In my view, this is indeed the first major decision to make when entering high-frequency trading. From a macro perspective, the two have many similarities: both inherently have extremely high infrastructure requirements and are highly sensitive to latency. However, in many key aspects, they exhibit opposite emphases: market making relies more on infrastructure capabilities; order taking relies more on statistical and mathematical modeling. I believe the choice of which path to take depends primarily on the type of work and research you prefer to invest in. Taking market making as an example, you are to some extent constrained by counterparties who "push through" the price, leaving very little room for error. Typically, using leverage and placing orders across multiple instruments and price levels creates significant implicit risk exposure; once an error occurs, the cost of tail risk is often very high. In contrast, a taker strategy can be triggered only once a day—and can still be an effective high-frequency strategy, possibly based on news or a specific type of signal. Because of the fewer triggers, you have the space to refine your model: not triggering most of the time doesn't matter, as long as the performance is good enough when it does. Conversely, market making lacks this flexibility—even if it operates well 99% of the time, a mere 1% lag or failure to keep up with the data can wipe out the entire PnL for the remaining 99%. This is the fundamental difference between "infrastructure-driven" and "model-driven" approaches. Corey Hoffstein: Could it be understood more intuitively like this: the party choosing to "take orders" is willing to cross the bid-ask spread because they expect the price to continue moving in their direction, thus willing to bear the spread cost; while the party "making markets" hopes the price will remain as stable as possible within their trading window—when someone crosses the spread to trade with them, they then hedge or reverse the trade on the other side to profit from the spread. Is this distinction reasonable? That is, one party prefers a sideways market within their trading window, while the other prefers directional movement. Jeff Yan: Yes, that's basically how it works. In high-frequency trading, we usually evaluate markout (look-back profits) using extremely short time windows, but this judgment also holds true for more general trading frequencies: as long as you choose to "take a bite," at the moment measured at the midpoint, you will immediately incur a certain loss (spread and fees). Only if, subsequently, within your set prediction time window, the average price movement can cover this immediate loss and further compensate for the fees, will your strategy have a positive expected value. Market making is exactly the opposite: at the moment of execution, your "initial PnL" is often at the highest level that the trade can achieve—because you have just captured a spread. What you are betting on is that this portion of the profit, on average, will not be completely eroded by "deteriorating selection." Therefore, in a market scenario, if all transactions are observed using markout over time, a decrease in PnL over time is usually the more common pattern; your expectation is simply that the decrease should not become negative. Corey Hoffstein: Before our call, you mentioned that the most difficult part of scaling up a business is not research, but infrastructure. I also saw a similar statement on X: "Being able to do data normalization doesn't guarantee you'll make money, but not being able to will definitely prevent you from making money." Could you talk about your biggest lessons learned regarding infrastructure, and why it's so crucial? Jeff Yan: This question can be roughly divided into two parts, and the two are closely related: one is "transaction infrastructure," and the other is "research infrastructure." Data cleaning leans more towards the latter, falling under the umbrella of statistical practice; the former refers to high-frequency trading systems in a narrower sense. Both are extremely important. While more well-known in research, it's crucial to emphasize that the signal-to-noise ratio and noise patterns in high-frequency trading are orders of magnitude worse than in most academic studies, making outlier handling far more critical. Without a proper framework for addressing these issues, simply ignoring outliers can lead to model failure in the event of a black swan event; conversely, without proper normalization or filtering, extreme samples can dominate model training and parameter selection. In practice, using quantiles is often more robust than using raw values ​​in many tasks; even when using raw values, a clear trade-off must be made between discarding and pruning outliers, and these choices often have a significant impact on the final result. The biggest lesson sounds simple: you must personally review the data. Don't assume that just because you're smart enough or your pipeline is "clean" enough, the model input will automatically meet expectations. The time spent checking raw data can hardly be considered "excessive"—because almost every review brings new discoveries. Early on, teams should completely dump all raw data streams provided by the exchange to disk, check them line by line, proactively identify anomalies, and perform consistency checks. Here's a seemingly absurd but real case: At one point, an exchange had a bug in its market data push, swapping the "price" and "quantity" fields. For example, Bitcoin's 20,000 / 0.1 was recorded as 0.1 / 20,000, causing our internal statistical and counting logic to be completely distorted. Many teams had to shut down or switch to backup data sources as a result. These types of events illustrate that no matter how "robust" your logic design is, it's impossible to cover all abnormal situations. Therefore, it's crucial to maintain closeness to and traceability of the original data as much as possible. Furthermore, pay close attention to timestamps. Exchanges often provide multiple timestamps in their data, and their true meaning needs to be analyzed and aligned manually. This is particularly critical for understanding "black box latency"—what exactly are you measuring? Are you truly "keeping up" with the market, or is the other party pushing low-quality data? By breaking down and comparing timestamps, these situations can be better distinguished, thereby determining whether the link is healthy and whether the latency is within a controllable range. What is "fair price"? How is it measured, and why does high-frequency market making revolve around it? Jeff Yan: Different trading firms do have different definitions of "fair," often depending on their respective trading styles. However, they share a common thread: fair essentially condenses your modeling results into a "predicted price." This abstraction is invaluable because it breaks down "how to build a profitable strategy" into two equally challenging tasks: price prediction and order execution. This also echoes your previous question about market making and order taking: market making leans more towards the execution side, while order taking leans more towards the modeling side. For order taking strategies, research and decision-making almost entirely revolve around the "fair price." As for what information should be included in the fair price, it depends on where you believe you have an advantage in data processing and where the market's efficiency gaps specifically lie. Furthermore, there isn't necessarily only one fair price. In a more machine learning-oriented framework, you can maintain fair prices for different prediction horizons simultaneously, such as a 1-second prediction and a 1-day prediction; execution strategies will be utilized in different ways, and the corresponding optimization objectives may differ along the PnL dimension. For beginners, a relatively effective "coarse-cut" approach is to first give a single value around which you are willing to quote prices or cross price spreads, and treat it as your "oracle"; then, with historical price sequences available, further consider how to achieve optimal execution around that value. Corey Hoffstein: Could it be simplified to this: Observing a certain exchange, if we assume Binance gathers almost all the liquidity, then Binance's price can be regarded as the fair price; if other exchanges (such as OKX) have a lag of milliseconds to seconds, then we can trade across the price difference based on Binance's fair price and wait for it to "catch up"? Of course, there are also more statistical approaches, that is, not taking a single exchange as the "true value", but combining relevant signals from the order book to estimate the fair price. Is this explanation valid? I'm not entirely sure either. Jeff Yan: Yes, the idea is correct. Using the most liquid exchange as the fair price is indeed a good first approximation. In the early days, price differences between exchanges often reached 10%. The main challenge then wasn't price prediction, but rather how to efficiently transfer funds between exchanges; therefore, this method was very effective at the time. In recent years, the market has undergone an evolution: liquidity was initially dispersed, then flowed back and concentrated on Binance (especially recently). Therefore, as you said, using Binance prices as a fair price is a reasonable starting point. However, it's important to emphasize that directly equating a single external source with a fair price requires caution. For example, OKX's lag might only be a few milliseconds, and actual transactions may not be as straightforward as described. Furthermore, suppose there's an opportunity: whenever the Binance price changes while OKX has no buy orders, you follow the trend and attempt to close your position for arbitrage—this might work in most cases, but this is the crypto market, and there's the risk of discontinuity: for example, OKX might suddenly go into wallet maintenance, temporarily severing deposits and withdrawals between Binance and OKX, preventing the arbitrage chain from closing, and potentially causing price divergence. If your fair price relies solely on the Binance price, you might face passive risk exposure. Therefore, there are many details involved. Even within this seemingly intuitive framework, it's far more complex than simply "taking a number from a data source as the fair price"—it can only serve as a good first approximation. Corey Hoffstein: This leads to my next question: the many technical characteristics and "traps" of crypto exchanges. Historically, their technical reputation has been inconsistent: you mentioned examples of "dirty data" (such as swapping price and quantity fields), API crashes, poor documentation, hidden endpoints, and even undocumented parameters. I remember you recently gave an example on X: it's possible to bypass the risk control engine or make it run in parallel—these completely undocumented details constitute "orthogonal alpha" that is unrelated to price prediction and independent of each other. My question is: how much alpha does work such as deeply understanding API details and accurately measuring endpoint latency actually contribute? Compared to this, which is more important: more "traditional" statistical alpha (such as using order book signals to determine pressure and direction)?

Jeff Yan: I remember the tweet you mentioned did have a good response.

... Corey Hoffstein: By the way, I'm still not sure if that was an April Fool's joke. Jeff Yan: April Fool's Day is over, and I admit it was a joke. But it's closer to reality than most people think. The real "joke" is that it's actually partly true. I've been wanting to write a follow-up, and this is a good reminder—I'll post it after I finish this episode. Back to your intuition, I think you're on the right track. People who work at a company for a long time often develop preferences; or they enter with preferences—for example, "I studied math, so I should build cooler machine learning models, mine signals, and produce alpha; that's the key, because that's the hardest part." This "model-only" approach might work in large companies because the division of labor is highly specialized; however, if you need to run the entire business independently, this approach alone won't get you far. The "dirty work" you mentioned—thoroughly understanding the API, filling in documentation gaps, and measuring latency at each endpoint—is crucial. My understanding of high-frequency trading (and many other things) is that it's more like the product of multiple factors than a simple addition. Your input in different "buckets" may seem summed, but the output often reflects a multiplicative relationship. For example: Overall efficiency ≈ Infrastructure × Model. If the "infrastructure" factor is only 1, while the "modeling" factor is 10, then for every unit of effort invested, the rational choice is often to prioritize addressing the weakest link. The difficulty in high-frequency trading lies in the fact that it's hard to accurately judge the level of each of these factors. Therefore, continuous "meta-analysis" is necessary in practice—am I doing the most important thing right now? You'll quickly find that the answer isn't obvious. Many competitive advantages lie precisely in the ability to prioritize. In this sense, seemingly "dirty work" is often crucial. One should pragmatically reap the low-hanging fruit, following the 80/20 rule. When the market is favorable, the easiest pitfall is: "The foundation is solid; I can do some cooler machine learning research and pursue innovation." We've paid the price for this before. This doesn't mean that alpha doesn't exist in this direction, but rather that the investment is large and the marginal returns often diminish rapidly. When your team is small, your existing strategies are still effective, and market opportunities are still plentiful, you need to repeatedly ask yourself and be honest about what should be your top priority right now. Don't be "induced" by superficial data to chase directions that shouldn't be your priority at the moment. Corey Hoffstein: For those who want to engage in high-frequency trading in the crypto space, you've suggested two paths: one is to trade directly on Binance and focus on generating alpha (I understand this is more like "actively taking orders" rather than "market making"); the other is to choose an exchange with long-tail characteristics, deeply understand its infrastructure "features," and find advantages accordingly. Could you further explain why you believe these are the two optimal paths? What are the methodological differences between the two? Jeff Yan: This can be compared to the intuitive conclusion of a "bell curve"—don't stay in the middle. If we understand the horizontal axis of the bell curve as different exchanges, then the most prominent problems are often in the middle range, roughly corresponding to platforms in the 2nd to 7th tiers. Their trading volume is much smaller than Binance's, but the intensity of competition is similar to "toxic traffic," and the traffic quality may even be worse. At least on Binance, we know that its retail traffic accounts for a very high proportion, which brings a "buffering effect"—a more favorable mix of toxic and retail traffic. Top-tier HFT companies have already fully integrated with the top 15 exchanges (you can roughly understand this as the top 15), and will be trading at full capacity using larger-scale and more mature strategies; it's difficult to squeeze much profit out of these mid-tier platforms. If you're willing to challenge yourself with highly scalable large-scale CEX strategies, start directly with Binance, and generalize as much as possible—there's no reason to start from the "middle." The other path you mentioned also holds true: go to the far left of the bell curve. Look for small, overlooked opportunities—either too small for large players to spend time on, or too niche for them to cover. Niche infrastructure is a prime example. Exchange systems are developed and implemented by people. Just as many DEX protocol designs may have obvious flaws, the technical implementations of some small centralized exchanges may also have clearly identifiable defects. If you truly understand the "uniqueness" of its operating mechanism, this itself can translate into a strategic advantage. Infrastructure is often a significant source of alpha, and there isn't an absolutely clear boundary between "model vs. infrastructure." You might worry about "non-generalization": for example, you might master a specific "exploitation method" on a small exchange, but it won't directly help on Binance. I believe the outside world generally underestimates the value of "running an effective strategy." For most teams, this should be the primary goal; the size of the strategy doesn't necessarily need to be overly concerned in the initial stages. Of course, there's a basic premise: if the platform is so small that there's almost no trading volume, research and deployment are meaningless. But as long as there's a certain trading volume, some returns can usually be achieved. More importantly, if the strategy has a high Sharpe ratio and is robust enough to handle retail events, the skills and experience you gain will be something most participants don't possess. Even if a specific strategy may not be directly generalizable, my experience is that as long as you successfully navigate the "research-launch-production" loop, the insights gained in this process often far exceed expectations; even if you subsequently scrap everything and switch to leading platforms like Binance, the overall difficulty will significantly decrease. Furthermore, while many detailed differences cannot be transferred one-to-one, you will begin to extract common principles from "proven effective methods" and continuously generate new ideas; these ideas are usually significantly better than ideas from scratch. Therefore, both paths have their value. If you find it difficult to choose, you can start small and gradually move to larger ones; frankly, trying both paths is also worthwhile. Corey Hoffstein: You mentioned "toxic traffic." Could you give a definition for someone who has never heard of this concept? Jeff Yan: Essentially, it's "information-dominated traffic." I have a framework for understanding the growth of the crypto market: I entered the market relatively late, so I can only imagine the earlier stages by looking back. Even when I entered, retail funds were already considerable, and there were large participants, but the core contradiction in supply and demand was that available liquidity was insufficient to meet retail trading needs. Therefore, retail traffic was the most direct and worthwhile target. The most intuitive approach was to develop a relatively universal market-making strategy that provided liquidity through order placement. As long as retail investors matched your orders, you could largely retain the profits they contributed across price spreads; at the time, this model itself was consistently profitable. This, in turn, sent a strong signal: the dominant market traffic at the time still primarily came from the retail side. However, as time went on, market participants gradually realized this and began to deploy market-making strategies on a large scale. As liquidity on the market-making side increased, the significance of taker strategies rose, and the bid-ask spread was continuously compressed. To continue capturing high-quality retail traffic, takers emerged and became more selective in "screening" the inferior orders on the market-making side, taking them away one by one. This is a common path in market evolution. It should be added that takers also provide significant value to the market; a simple dichotomy of "market making = market maker, takers = counterparty" is inaccurate, as these two roles often intertwine in practice. In my view, a more ideal market form allows participants to trade freely in their own way. From a market maker's perspective, however, this type of order-taking traffic significantly increases the difficulty of the strategy: the previously relatively easy model—continuously placing orders and earning a small spread each time they are filled—can be "drained" by a few trades. For example, you might accumulate a profit of about 1 basis point in about 99% of retail trades, but lose 10 basis points in a single trade in about 1% of trades (this is just a mental model, not an exact number). In this structure, tail losses are enough to eat up most of the regular profits. Therefore, "toxic traffic" largely refers to this type of traffic represented by order takers who have an informational advantage. Of course, whether something constitutes "toxic" depends on the specific strategy you're running; but in most contexts, a relatively intuitive distinction can be made between "retail traffic" and "institutionalized/high-level traffic." How common are "adversarial algorithms" used to lure HFTs in the crypto market? Jeff Yan: Crypto does indeed have a "Wild West" feel. From a more positive perspective, crypto is also an experiment, and stance and perspective are particularly important. Regulators often seize on one point—"They haven't followed our carefully crafted securities laws." DeFi proponents, on the other hand, argue that these securities laws themselves are likely influenced by lobbying and human judgment; crypto might offer a more libertarian experimental space: what exactly must be regulated? I'm not entirely sure either; reality is likely somewhere in between. I am neither a regulator nor a policymaker; I'm simply sharing some philosophical observations. Returning to the practical level, trading in the crypto market will be extremely difficult if you don't pay attention to manipulative and exploitative strategies. Another reality is that it's not that exchanges are unwilling to be regulated, but rather that it's often unclear who should regulate which exchange—at least for me, this is unclear. Many legal frameworks differ significantly between countries, which is perhaps one of the important reasons why this problem persists. Furthermore, operating an exchange is extremely difficult in itself; they also need to handle a large number of other matters simultaneously. To give a more concrete example: spoofing (deceptive order placement/false reporting) is a very common practice. I don't intend to delve into its strict technical definition under US securities and futures law; the term "spoofing" here leans towards a broader meaning: from the order book and the subsequent price action, you can often clearly observe that someone places a huge number of orders, but obviously has no real intention to execute them—in fact, they might even feel disadvantaged if the orders were executed. Although it's difficult to prove their "intention" legally, these orders are clearly not for execution, but to create the illusion of abundant orders on one side. The result is that if some algorithms interpret order book liquidity as a price trend signal, they may be misled and place orders in the corresponding direction. After the "induction" takes effect, the spoofing algorithm either places more easily targeted maker orders or actively absorbs the passive orders exposed by the induction. This kind of situation is very common. Another, more straightforward example is various forms of market manipulation, such as organized groups that "pump and dump." Out of observation, I have infiltrated several such groups, never participating in the trading, only observing. This phenomenon is quite large. Recently, much of this activity has indeed been cleaned up, which is a good thing; but in earlier years, they could even create exaggerated trading volumes: an "insider" would announce a token, followed by a rapid influx of retail users (their organization is unclear to me), and the insider would use the traffic to unload their holdings. For high-frequency trading, this scenario seems manageable on the surface, but it's actually very difficult to handle because the strong mean reversion effect often "lures" the strategy in the opposite direction. As for how to deal with it, it brings us back to the trade-offs you previously raised regarding infrastructure, models, and strategies—where should the effort be invested? For me, this type of issue falls under the category of "miscellaneous/special scenarios" that must be covered, and can also be categorized under risk management and special situation handling. In short, if this part of the work isn't done, even if other aspects are done almost perfectly, this part can still become the key factor determining the long-term average PnL success or failure in different market conditions and with different targets. Jeff Yan: We were truly shocked when we first encountered this situation. Looking back, we were lucky: initially, the targets we traded were either not easily manipulated, or the other party hadn't had time to act. We hadn't foreseen this problem at all, and built the system under the premise of "ignorance," and PnL progressed smoothly for a while. But once you get caught in the trap, the impact is very severe—if the strategy isn't constrained, you could lose a day's PnL in a minute. Sometimes automated trading is actually the most "foolish" type of trading because it's essentially just a finite state machine lacking human discretion, simply executing according to a preset path. Our approach was quite pragmatic: of course, you can sit down and analyze, model, to predict whether manipulation is occurring; but one of our advantages at the time was our extremely fast response, data-driven approach, and lack of obsession with the "most standard" path. For us, the approach was—once a specific loss pattern emerged, we would immediately shut down the relevant logic; Jeff Yan: These rules could often be written within an hour and deployed directly to the production environment. At the time, we strictly adhered to the 80/20 rule: this did mean missing some opportunities, but it also freed up time and energy to scale up and advance key projects that could amplify PnL tenfold, instead of being continuously bogged down by these problems. Perhaps about 5% of the time, we would forgo potential gains due to shutdowns, but this was essentially a trade-off and judgment—investing resources in the most valuable work. As resources and time became more abundant, we gradually deepened our understanding of this area: we now possess more complex models for predicting relevant market conditions and identifying ongoing behaviors; compared to the earlier, more "discrete" on/off processing, we now employ continuous parameter and weight adjustments to dynamically constrain and adaptively configure our strategies. To date, we have developed a relatively deep understanding of how these manipulative behaviors operate and their identifiable characteristics. However, it must be emphasized that for newcomers, the 80/20 rule remains the most important guiding principle. Is market manipulation more prevalent on long-tail cryptocurrencies and smaller exchanges? Jeff Yan: This is relatively rare on any exchange, especially Bitcoin and Ethereum, because they have much higher liquidity. I think it depends more on the asset itself than the exchange. I've seen (manipulation/deception) on almost every exchange; the methods differ across platforms, and you can sense that the participants aren't entirely the same, but the overall approach is largely consistent. There's a "sweet spot": if a token has almost no trading volume, it's usually not worth investing in; but it's different for some altcoins with a certain trading volume—the algorithm anticipates a certain level of trading and liquidity, creating an opportunity for manipulation that allows manipulators to profit. Corey Hoffstein: I've always believed that our way of observing the market is often constrained by our own trading cycles. As a high-frequency trader, your intuition about microstructures might be quite different from mine, who holds positions for longer periods and is more fundamentally oriented. You once tweeted that the market is like a viscous fluid, where external shocks manifest as damped oscillations during price discovery. I found this analogy very interesting; could you elaborate on it? Jeff Yan: I also value understanding the essence of things. This is probably related to my background in mathematics and physics—it's difficult for me to innovate on a "black box" system without understanding its underlying mechanisms. Therefore, I tend to construct mental analogies and metaphors to help understand how the market works. Using the "viscous fluid" model as an example, let's return to a more fundamental question: Why can high-frequency trading make money? Many retail investors view it as a form of "predation," for example, believing we are "rushing ahead" or "hunting stop-loss orders." I'm not claiming that high-frequency trading is "doing good," but I believe it does provide a necessary service to the market to some extent. External factors influencing prices can be abstracted as "shocks" exerted on the system (which, for us, are largely random): for example, someone urgently needs to execute a trade and must immediately obtain liquidity; or a news event changes the "fair value" of an asset. Although some may try to interpret the event itself, such demands are often sudden and usually "exit immediately after execution." The order book is essentially a strong PvP (player versus player) arena, with many participants entering with a clear sense of urgency; and a feedback loop forms: momentum trading triggers more trading, leading to various unstable equilibria. In this structure, prices often experience a large initial shock before market participants gradually "enter" and engage in a game around the true fair price. The first jump is usually the largest; afterwards, some will judge that "an overshoot has occurred" and engage in mean-reversion trading—this could come from mid- to high-frequency participants, for example, believing that "the price will fall back on the mean in the next 5 seconds." At the same time, some will believe the event has a far-reaching impact and choose to push prices higher until a larger increase occurs; for example, an event like "Elon adding Doge to Twitter" might be seen as having "real impact" within their narrative framework, thus conversely driving down positions on the mean-reversion side. Overall, this is more like a "price vote" and continuous game played with real money. Its key characteristic is that the volatility gradually converges. As participants gradually build their target positions, funds continuously complete a weighted average, and prices eventually tend to converge to a more stable fair price range. In this process, the core function of high-frequency trading remains providing liquidity by buying low and selling high. If the price path is viewed as a fluctuating curve, high-frequency trading buys when the curve is low and sells when it is high. Its trading impact, on average, has a smoothing effect on the curve—causing prices to approach the fair price more quickly and to operate around the fair price as much as possible during the price formation process. Therefore, within this analogy, the stronger the high-frequency trading capability and the more abundant the market liquidity, the higher the viscosity (stronger the damping) of this "fluid." This mental model may not be rigorous, but it largely conveys the meaning I intended in that tweet. Why can high-frequency trading P&L predict mid-frequency price changes? Jeff Yan: This is some of our internal "exploratory ideas." As I mentioned before, iterating around proven directions is almost always better: higher accuracy and easier to scale. But we also reserve room for a few bolder explorations, which occasionally do produce results. This time it was a relatively successful "interest project," and at the beginning, we didn't have strong prior judgments. The motivation was primarily that our available capital had exceeded the capacity that high-frequency strategies could effectively support; although we had connected to multiple exchanges, this was largely a constant-term expansion with diminishing marginal returns—because the size of the platforms we subsequently connected to was getting smaller and smaller. Therefore, we began to consider whether we could extend our reach to the mid-frequency trading domain—ideally, an "ideal asset" with a Sharpe ratio of 3–4 and a capacity hundreds of times greater than high-frequency strategies. This idea sounded extremely attractive. However, we generally accepted the basic framework of an efficient market. Yes, we have an advantage in high-frequency trading, but if given a set of daily data and asked to predict daily returns, it would be difficult for us to find a reliable entry point directly. Based on this cautious approach, this "brainstorming" idea provides a relatively feasible path: in mid-frequency trading, obtaining data sources that are valuable to others but inaccessible to them can itself create a strategic advantage. We cannot obtain "alternative data" like some institutions, such as satellite imagery or parking lot traffic statistics. So, what do we truly possess? We possess our own HFT PnL—a private data set, clearly not random noise; its time-series format reveals certain structural characteristics, making it worthy of further study. Further questioning, what factors is it related to? Returning to the previous discussion about "toxic traffic and retail traffic," it is highly correlated with retail traffic. A relatively simple priori assumption is that if you can distinguish the types of market participants and understand their behavioral patterns, you can often obtain better signals. The overall priori assumption remains that "most signals lack stable predictability," but the direction is not necessarily clear. Therefore, our approach is: since we possess this indicator, and it is related to retail traffic, which in turn is probabilistically related to price formation—we should thoroughly explore this path and conduct rigorous analysis and verification. Jeff Yan: We did indeed conduct this analysis. The overall approach is to incorporate a series of features centered on P&L (such as the change in P&L, the "derivative" of P&L, etc.) into a regression framework to predict price performance at a mid-frequency scale under different time windows. Initially, we were unsure how to implement mid-frequency research, so we adopted a relatively "broad coverage" approach: starting with 5-minute returns and then gradually expanding the time scale to several hours. Jeff Yan: Our research primarily relies on our internal dashboard data system, which aggregates P&L values ​​for different strategies across different exchanges and assets, and supports segmentation by exchange/strategy/asset. Due to significant data noise, robust processing is necessary; obviously, we wouldn't directly use the P&L of a single coin to regress its mid-frequency trend—the noise is too high, and interpretability is limited. We basically follow the 80/20 principle, using methods such as binning and grouping to avoid obvious overfitting and adhere to existing priors, resulting in a rather interesting and counterintuitive conclusion: Whether for market making or order taking, the PnL on the high-frequency side is significantly negatively correlated with subsequent returns of crypto assets, and the effect is not weak. We were initially very excited when trying to capture this effect in live trading: within a 1-2 hour prediction window, the magnitude of the effect reached tens of basis points, and the volume was relatively high. However, the problem is that this signal almost exclusively suggests shorting, lacking a symmetrical reverse effect (theoretically, this might exist, but our strategy iterations avoid prolonged periods of continuous losses). In other words, when we are making money, the model's meaning is closer to: shorting is advisable. Jeff Yan: So, what exactly should we short? Intuitively, it should be perpetual contracts or futures. But in practice, two real-world constraints arise. The first is funding rates. When such situations occur, many experienced participants are also shorting; even if they focus on different underlying signals, alpha may be highly correlated, market behavior tends to move in the same direction, and funding rates will reflect and absorb this congestion. Secondly, the individual instruments with the most "significantly effective" signals are often extreme examples, and these instruments are actually difficult to short in practice, possibly due to insufficient liquidity, limited securities lending, or incomplete contract instruments. Nevertheless, the overall effect remains usable. High-frequency trading naturally creates inventory, which you can hedge or internalize between strategies; even without internalization, you can apply a bias to inventory targets when the signal is strongest (e.g., minimize or avoid positions), thus contributing positively to overall P&L. Regarding the idea of ​​"abstracting a single, reusable mid-frequency short-selling strategy," we believe it lacks persuasiveness and therefore did not package it as an independent strategy. This is the closest type of alpha to being publicly shared; however, its feasibility still depends on your strategy portfolio and trading process design. It can certainly be transformed into a viable alpha within a specific system. Corey Hoffstein: I really like this idea: directly shorting in futures may not be feasible because funding rates may have already partially priced in the signals; but adjusting inventory bias to absorb this alpha is an alternative path and can have a substantial impact on P&L. This reminds me of some practices in my frequency band—for example, DFA (Distributed Averaging Capital) doesn't explicitly trade momentum, but when buying value stocks, they exclude those with significantly weak momentum: instead of directly using momentum as a factor to build a position, they wait for the negative momentum phase to subside on a completely different time scale before entering the value sector. This is similar to the logic here: theoretically "orthogonal" alpha is not expressed as an explicit position, but rather integrated into the trading process, continuously improving results through marginal advantages and subtle improvements. This concept is very inspiring. Jeff Yan: One more point. Your example was interesting. I hadn't encountered it before, but I had heard some "human traders" who primarily trade with large positions mention similar practices: in the crypto market, they trigger actions whenever the 50-day moving average crosses above or below another moving average ("golden cross"/"death cross"). This means their core decision-making doesn't rely on technical analysis, but they treat a certain technical condition as a trigger. I haven't specifically studied the case you mentioned, but it reminded me of a similar approach—waiting for a seemingly reliable "conditional signal" to change before executing a predetermined trading process. Corey Hoffstein: Yes, essentially it's waiting for a conditional signal to change. Very interesting. We've talked a lot about centralized exchanges, but not much about on-chain strategies/decentralized exchanges. You mentioned that your favorite on-chain strategy, which you've since discontinued, was RFQ (RFQ). Could you explain what it was, why you liked it so much and why it worked so well, and why you stopped? Jeff Yan: About six months ago, we started increasing our investment in DeFi. At that time, the industry generally believed that the best opportunities were migrating to on-chain, while centralized exchanges had entered a phase of diminishing marginal returns (overall trading activity was low). Therefore, we decided to invest more time in researching DeFi. During that period, RFQs (Request for Quotes) became a hot topic. CrocSwap's Douglas recently posted some interesting tweets, and I largely agree with his point: this design is not ideal—it attempts to directly transplant mechanisms that work in traditional finance (TradFi) to DeFi, but it may not be suitable for the on-chain environment. To help unfamiliar listeners understand, let's first provide some background: The starting point of RFQs is quite clear—to help market makers filter out "toxic traffic" and allow retail users to directly connect with market makers. A retail user initiates a request: "I am a retail user, please give me a quote." The market maker returns a quote (usually better than the spread, or at least able to meet the retail user's desire for a larger transaction size). After receiving the quote signed by the market maker, the retail user broadcasts the signed payload to a smart contract; after the contract verifies the signature's validity, the settlement of assets between the two parties is completed. Its essence is closer to a "protocol-based OTC" mechanism. This idea does sound reasonable and is quite common in traditional finance (TradFi): users can obtain larger-scale transactions that are less likely to be "front-loaded" by high-frequency trading, which is a better service for the retail side. However, in the DeFi context, this is almost an obvious design flaw because you can't prove the other party is indeed a "retail investor"—on-chain anonymity is the default, and there's no KYC layer of identity verification. To verify this, we wrote a very simple Python script to send out batch price requests. The market maker did indeed provide excellent quotes: the spread was only about 5 basis points, and the quotes were valid for 60–90 seconds. In most cases, from the market maker's perspective, obtaining such a trade should be attractive; the trade size they were willing to offer was also quite substantial (in the hundreds of thousands of dollars). But this also directly exposed the mechanism's flaw: in a system where identity cannot be verified and anonymity is the default, anyone can pretend to be a "retail investor" and exploit the pricing mechanism to gain an advantage. This is the fundamental reason why we initially liked this type of on-chain RFQ strategy but quickly stopped using it. Jeff Yan: Our approach is actually quite simple: we wait for price fluctuations. The crypto market is inherently volatile, and once the price changes, we broadcast the signed transaction to the blockchain. The counterparty has limited options. This strategy has a very high Sharpe ratio. Furthermore, you don't even need to wait for price fluctuations to trigger it—it's essentially closer to a "free option" with a clear time value: you can wait until the quote is close to expiration before deciding whether to submit the trade. Jeff Yan: This also makes the returns more stable. That's how we executed it. Obviously, we weren't the only participants doing this (we might have been one of them), and market makers quickly reacted: they stopped providing us with normal quotes, arguing, "You're making us lose money; you're clearly not retail investors," and either offered extremely wide spreads or simply stopped quoting. We can also address this, for example, by changing the address or wallet to continue the request. From a principle perspective, I don't think there's a problem with this strategy itself. It's more of a practical concern: the greatest value of implementing this strategy might lie in demonstrating to the market that the microstructure design of RFQs has inherent flaws—capital and intellectual resources should be redirected to a more rational mechanism. Perhaps in this sense, our "experiment" has already fulfilled its function. I understand that many current RFQ mechanisms have introduced a "last look" for market makers, rather than allowing retail investors to retain the final decision-making power. As you said, we later stopped this type of strategy. I do think it's an evolution; but once market makers are given a last look, the core advantages of RFQs are essentially weakened or even disappear. This point can also be seen in related discussions on Twitter: it's extremely difficult to outperform a Centralized Limit Book (CLOB) at the mechanism level, and I don't believe RFQs can reliably achieve this in DeFi. The above experience further illustrates that after repeated trial and error, we increasingly felt that this field was still immature, and the mechanism design of many protocols was not fully thought out. Against this backdrop, we made a strategic judgment at the time: rather than "arbitrage-style adaptation" to existing mechanisms, it would be better for us to build a platform truly geared towards retail users and capable of decentralized price discovery. Why the Switch to Hyperliquid? Jeff Yan: The reason we decided to get involved was because we encountered a strong sense of confusion in DeFi transactions: even during the DeFi downturn in mid-2022, retail traffic was still considerable, but users were experiencing extremely poor user experiences with protocols. They were paying high gas fees due to the poor performance of the underlying public chains, while also using solutions with flawed mechanisms (such as RFQs). Even more surprisingly, users were willing to continue using them; the data clearly showed that the demand always existed. Based on this judgment, we conducted further in-depth research. I don't quite remember the exact location of the FTX incident on this timeline, but it should have been shortly before its collapse. After the FTX collapse, the market narrative quickly shifted to counterparty risk: phrases like "Not your keys, not your coins," which were previously more like slogans, suddenly became the most pressing concerns for most people. This further reinforced our belief that "we should build some kind of infrastructure." However, we repeatedly weighed what exactly to build: first, we needed to understand the real needs of users and the unmet needs in the market. At that time, there were already numerous clones of swaps on the market, with various minor innovations and aggregators emerging one after another, and different curves and formulas creating a huge parameter space. However, we were not optimistic about the AMM route: a significant portion of the liquidity generated by so-called "market making" was low-quality liquidity driven by erroneous or misleading narratives (such as the way "impermanent loss" was described, and the legacy effects of liquidity mining). Even if AMMs did represent the main market demand, this track was already highly crowded, and the incremental value we could provide by launching another similar product was unclear. Therefore, we turn our attention back to centralized exchanges: What do users truly need? Where does price discovery primarily occur? Where is effective liquidity concentrated? The answers are remarkably consistent—perpetual contracts. Perpetual contracts are an extremely ingenious innovation (the idea can be traced back to traditional markets, but it has been fully developed in the crypto market). In the decentralized space, there are almost no projects that truly provide this capability in a decentralized manner. dYdX, while using an order book structure, still relies on centralized matching; it is the closest approach, but that's about it. Our conclusion is: since the gap is clear, let's fill it. The value proposition for traders is also straightforward: if you appreciate the centralized trading experience of Binance and Bybit, but are unwilling to bear the risks of custody—then Hyperliquid is trying to provide precisely that option. Hyperliquid recently launched its closed beta test, aiming to provide a trading experience consistent with centralized exchanges (CEXs): sufficiently narrow spreads, near-instant order confirmation, and almost zero gas costs (used solely for DoS defense). Under congestion-free conditions, its blockchain can process tens of thousands of orders per second. All actions are fully transparent and recorded on-chain, with all operations presented as on-chain transaction records—this is our vision. Our primary target users are the DeFi community, because educating a wider audience that "you can do without custodians and entrust your trust to on-chain contracts" is costly and not our forte; while DeFi users are already willing to use it today. What we aim to do is demonstrate to them that most protocols are not serious; some are merely stopgap measures/band-aid solutions, or temporary mechanisms based on local prices—suitable for gamblers, but not for serious traders who need real liquidity and reliable price discovery. What we offer is usable liquidity and a decentralized price discovery mechanism. Blockchain and smart contracts themselves can assume escrow and settlement functions, establishing verifiable trust at the mechanism level. However, promoting this concept is not easy, nor is it our forte. Our strategy is more direct: show the difference with products and facts, letting users see that among the complex choices of protocols, most solutions are not rigorous, many are just short-term patches (some even based solely on local prices). These mechanisms may be more suitable for "Degen-style" participation than for professional traders who need a stable trading experience and real liquidity. The difference with Hyperliquid is that we designed it from the very beginning around these needs. To this end, we made significant technological innovations and dedicated most of a quarter to intensive development. Initially, we were also attracted to dYdX's approach: off-chain matching and on-chain non-custodial settlement. However, after further analysis, we believed that this model had a structural flaw—the degree of decentralization of a system depends on its most centralized link. Based on this judgment, we could not accept this solution because it was difficult to scale to our envisioned scale and vision. Therefore, we returned to our original goal: complete decentralization was essential. Under our constraints, this almost meant there was no other choice—we had to develop our own public chain. We were not inclined to take detours, nor did we blindly follow existing conclusions. It's widely believed that building your own L1 blockchain is extremely difficult. Our approach is to focus on resolving the consensus issue first. Tendermint isn't perfect, but it's highly mature and has been extensively tested in real-world scenarios. We chose to build upon it and have progressed to our current stage. Why Hyperliquid chose to build its own L1 blockchain and consider it a key "vertical integration" decision? Jeff Yan: Over the past few years, L1 has become a significant narrative in the industry, with many large investments revolving around it, such as Solana and Avalanche, so-called L1 projects. The concept itself isn't complicated: L1 refers to a blockchain entity. In contrast, there's the "smart contract-based implementation path"—that is, implementing exchange logic through smart contracts on top of another existing L1 blockchain (such as Ethereum or Solana), with that L1 responsible for execution and settlement. This is important because it involves a rather subtle incentive structure. Many teams are willing to "build on a particular L1," partly because it makes it easier to gain support and promotional resources from VCs/funds holding large amounts of tokens; and the value of general-purpose smart contract L1s also depends on the application ecosystem, so they naturally tend to attract developers to "deploy contracts on my platform." In contrast, the Cosmos chain based on Tendermint is closer to a self-sovereign model—lacking strong external incentives to drive its expansion, and its value does not directly flow back to a single entity. From my personal experience (we've tried both paths), it's hard to imagine building a truly high-quality exchange simply by using an existing L1 as a general-purpose contract platform, especially in derivatives scenarios, and especially in order book mode. In a sense, dYdX provides further evidence: as a recognized pioneer, it also chose to switch to its own blockchain after five years of operation. Their motivations may include legal pressure (I can only speculate), but in any case, their current architecture is clearly not fully decentralized; once the new chain is ready, the old architecture will gradually be phased out. For us, if the goal is to build a truly high-quality exchange, taking the L1 path is a more orthodox and scalable direction. To give a more concrete example: if an exchange is fully implemented as a "smart contract," it will inevitably be strongly constrained by the rules of the underlying contract platform. Taking Ethereum as an example, transactions and state updates usually need to be triggered by user transactions. Therefore, for one of the most basic operational actions of a perpetual contract exchange—such as settling funding fees every 8 hours—once there are 100,000 open "sessions/positions" in the system, the number of storage slots that need to be updated cannot be accommodated within a single block. You'll then have to design an additional mechanism for "who triggers the fund fee settlement": this might require auctioning trigger permissions, designing incentives and fee allocation, and subsidizing gas costs for the triggerer. More importantly, this process often cannot be completed atomically, ultimately resulting in "settlement approximately every 8 hours," but the specific execution time depends on the activity level of participants at the time, potentially delayed by several minutes. For strategic traders, it's difficult to establish a stable execution and risk control framework around this uncertainty. This type of operation is a "basic action" for all perpetual contract exchanges. If implemented on a self-developed blockchain, it can be significantly simplified: the fund fee settlement logic can be written into the consensus protocol. For example, it can be stipulated that when a new block is produced and its timestamp is exactly a multiple of 8 hours since genesis, the system automatically triggers fund fee settlement and executes the corresponding logic, making the overall implementation more direct and controllable. In other words, operating a perpetual contract exchange is, in essence, closer to building an L1 blockchain than simply writing a few smart contracts. Why does Hyperliquid believe that order book DEXs are superior to the "tiered fee" liquidity pool model? Jeff Yan: The tiered fee aspect is indeed quite representative. You'll see that many AMMs are slowly "evolving" towards an order book model. For many DeFi practitioners, this inevitably brings a sense of frustration—as if they are ultimately reinventing the wheel. There may be some local innovations in the process, but from a deeper structural perspective, the liquidity pool model has its "ingenuity," but it also carries a sense of being somewhat "over-packaged." This is because AMMs largely stem from the computational and storage constraints of their time. Back in 2018 (when Uniswap first emerged), the on-chain arithmetic capabilities were extremely limited. A single transaction often only updated a very small amount of storage state, and users couldn't accept excessively high gas costs. AMM (Automated Market Maker) was a compromise solution developed to achieve "barely usable" operation under these highly limited computing and storage conditions. Its operation, in a sense, relied on persuading funds to enter the pool and provide liquidity. At the same time, packaging impermanent loss as a marketing narrative is, in my opinion, a very clever but also questionable approach: emphasizing to retail users that "depositing is not a transaction, but rather earning returns"; even if losses occur, they are explained as "impermanent" and negligible costs. Whether this narrative adequately reveals the risks is at least open to discussion. From a trader's perspective, arbitrage opportunities in AMM pools are relatively explicit in mechanism: arbitrage around these pools can generate profits. While such trading is now quite crowded, it was indeed a strategy with a clear positive expected value in its early stages. Unlike order book markets, liquidity providers in pools are often not professional market makers, but rather retail LPs. Many people deposit funds into the pools and then leave them unmanaged for extended periods, relying on so-called "mining rewards" to maintain participation. Without effective management and risk awareness, their expected returns may be less than ideal in the long run, and they may even continuously passively suffer losses from unfavorable choices. "Liquidity mining" initially forced liquidity in through incentives; as these incentives diminish, some funds may remain in the pools, and some participants may even lack sustained attention to their exposure. This structure itself is hardly sustainable. Some might argue that "trading volume remains high," but within your framework, this is more likely related to incentive and marketing mechanisms than a natural consequence of long-term equilibrium. In long-term equilibrium, truly affordable liquidity may gradually decrease until LPs, to cover the implicit costs of unfavorable choices, are forced to raise fees to higher levels; once fees rise, retail traffic is further suppressed, creating a negative feedback loop. At that level of "truly affordable" liquidity, calculating returns against risk costs often yields unsatisfactory results. This is one of the fundamental reasons why I believe the pooled liquidity model is difficult to sustain in the long run. Its so-called "upgraded version" can be understood as GMX and a series of "GMX clones": they no longer rely on constant function curves but instead use oracle prices. To ensure that oracle readings at the time of a transaction are as close as possible to the "true price," such protocols often incorporate numerous restrictions and expedient designs. However, even with these measures, problems frequently arise—for example, someone might manipulate prices on a centralized exchange and then profit by trading against the manipulated oracle quotes on GMX. Mechanistically, these methods are essentially more like "pain-relieving patches" than fundamentally addressing the issues of price discovery and counterparty information advantage. In my view, with recent advancements in underlying technologies such as L1 consensus, we no longer need to make excessive compromises between "decentralization" and "usable trading formats": maintaining decentralization while also supporting order book-based trading. Based on experience, this is almost the only fully validated path capable of achieving true price discovery and forming a "real market." Will self-built L1 slow down price discovery due to cross-chain and fiat currency deposits and withdrawals? Jeff Yan: This is indeed a common problem across the entire crypto industry, not just DeFi. Even when arbitrage is done on centralized exchanges, the deposit and withdrawal process still runs on the public chain. Once on-chain congestion occurs, transfer efficiency will significantly decrease. Our initial focus on perpetual contracts is essentially an 80/20 trade-off: the vast majority of trading volume and price discovery in the market are concentrated in perpetual contracts. Building on this, we do another 80/20 trade-off by first uniformly using USDC as collateral to establish the core pathway; then gradually introducing various stablecoins to diversify risk is not difficult. For most users, this model is smoother: after depositing USDC into the bridge/chain/contract system, they can express opinions and trade a large number of crypto assets in one place. Regarding the expression of opinions and price discovery for highly volatile assets, as long as collateral is available, positions can be established and arbitrage can be implemented. A typical scenario is spot-perpetual arbitrage: obtaining funding fees and trading the spot-futures price difference. Under this structure, the perpetual end can be completed entirely on Hyperliquid without frequently moving spot or USDC. Of course, the cross-chain issue itself still needs continuous attention. There are currently many noteworthy full-chain technology directions; we have integrated some of these solutions and will continue to support related evolutions. Due to resource and priority constraints, we will not personally invest in "original innovation" of multi-chain infrastructure, but the ultimate goal is clear: assets can come from any source chain and be staked through trusted minimization or decentralized bridging, thus being directly used as collateral for Hyperliquid. Currently, whether trying Hyperliquid through the UI, Python SDK, or directly calling the raw API, the end-to-end latency is roughly between 100 and 300 milliseconds; due to the randomness of block generation, this value is not entirely certain. You might think this is an order of magnitude slower than Binance's order placement latency. However, the impact of latency is not linearly cumulative like transaction fees; for our core user group that we prioritize serving—retail users—humans can hardly reliably distinguish the difference between 100 milliseconds and tens of milliseconds. Even if it's possible to distinguish them, it's not a key factor in most cases; what they care more about is obtaining "instant" interactive feedback. In typical market conditions, prices don't change substantially between 100 milliseconds and 10 milliseconds. For the vast majority of transaction scenarios, this latency introduced by block time can be approximated as zero, so running a self-developed L1 can basically keep it within an acceptable range.

data.title: Bitcoin Slides to $81K Nine-Month Low as Macro Fears Trigger $1.7B Liquidation Wave
data.description:
Bitcoin slid to a nine-month low of $81,058 early Friday, triggering a brutal wave of forced liquidations as traders rushed to de-risk amid mounting geopolitical tensions, fresh tariff threats from the White House, and renewed anxiety around the tech and AI sectors. The sharp drop marked Bitcoin’s weakest level since April and extended a deep pullback from its October all-time high of $126,000.

RIP to the people who sell their $BTC this low, only for it to bounce back in February...

It was their selling which has pleased the whales and allows them to continue accumulating while the price remains stagnant.

(So far #Bitcoin has defended $80k firmly. This would indicate… pic.twitter.com/4IWbe4lvkQ

— ₿ŁUΞBS CRYPTO (@0xBluebs) January 30, 2026

Data from CoinGlass shows that more than 270,000 traders were liquidated over the past 24 hours, with total losses reaching roughly $1.68 billion. The selloff was overwhelmingly concentrated in leveraged long positions, which accounted for around 93% of the liquidations, as Bitcoin and Ether both slid rapidly during thin early trading hours. Ether fell alongside Bitcoin to around $2,725, compounding losses across the broader crypto market.

The downturn pushed Bitcoin into a critical monthly support zone, intensifying volatility as automated liquidations cascaded across major derivatives platforms. At the same time, the wider crypto market suffered a sharp contraction, with roughly $200 billion wiped from total market capitalization in a single day, underscoring how quickly risk appetite has deteriorated.

🚨 $450 MILLION in longs just got wiped out.

The leverage flush is complete. The weak hands are out.

I'm not fading this dip. Opening a LONG on $BTC right now. 🟢

Enough thinking.#Bitcoin#Crypto#Trading#Liquidationpic.twitter.com/Gg084tB21K

— Next 100X GEMS (@Next100XGEMS) January 29, 2026

The selloff unfolded against a tense macro backdrop. Markets were rattled after the United States dispatched additional naval assets to the Middle East amid escalating tensions with Iran, with President Donald Trump warning that Washington was prepared to act if diplomacy failed. Trump also added to investor unease by declaring a national emergency and signing an executive order that would impose tariffs on goods from countries involved in supplying oil to Cuba, reviving fears of trade disruptions and retaliatory measures.

Risk sentiment deteriorated further after disappointing tech earnings reignited concerns about the sustainability of AI-driven growth. Microsoft shares plunged 10% on Thursday in their steepest one-day decline since March 2020, following signs of slowing cloud revenue growth despite record spending. Jeff Mei, chief operating officer at crypto exchange BTSE, said the crypto selloff showed a clear correlation with the tech sector’s weakness, as investors moved to reduce exposure across high-risk assets.

$MSFT s'est faite coupée hier, le narratif principal est la crainte des backlog lié à OpenAI.

Nous allons ce soir publier l'analyse des résultats de microsoft par notre agent IA, pour informer au mieux l'investisseur.#IA#Microsoft#Finance#bourse#Aurorapic.twitter.com/Pj5xnZW5UV

— Aurora (@AuroraFinanceAI) January 30, 2026

Traditional safe havens also failed to provide shelter. Gold fell roughly 9% from its recent high near $5,600 per ounce, while silver dropped more than 11%, suggesting that investors were prioritizing liquidity over protection as volatility spiked across global markets.

Gold just dropped from 5,600 → 5,097 in under an hour, wiping out trillions in market cap .
Manias don’t end with opportunity.
They end with inventory.

Miss the gold rush, and you don’t strike gold , you inherit the shovels. pic.twitter.com/OxWkgTvLQR

— The Moroccan Investor (@marocbourse) January 30, 2026

Despite the severity of the move, some analysts argue the decline may be overstated. Mei said cryptocurrencies have already been trending lower since October and now appear more reasonably valued, adding that downside could be limited if macro conditions stabilize. Still, traders are closely watching whether Bitcoin can hold above the $81,000 level, with a sustained break risking a deeper slide toward the mid-$70,000 range.

For now, Bitcoin’s plunge highlights its growing sensitivity to global macro forces, reinforcing its role as a high-beta asset rather than a defensive hedge during periods of heightened geopolitical and economic stress.

data.title: Why We're Bullish on On-Chain Perpetual US Stocks: From No Bank Account to No Brokerage Firm
data.description:
Author: YettaS Source: X, @YettaSing

As gold and silver continue to hit new highs, Trade.xyz's daily trading volume approaches $2 billion, and Binance almost without hesitation launched TSLA perpetual contracts, the trend can no longer be ignored: traditional financial assets are becoming a new gateway for the crypto market to absorb global liquidity. Just a year ago, most CEX operators probably couldn't accept the fact that an on-chain exchange could use TradFi assets as a wedge to begin directly eroding and reshaping the core territory of centralized exchanges.

We all know that crypto funds inherently favor volatility; from a product structure perspective, equity perp happens to be at the intersection of several key upgrades, which is the fundamental reason why it stands out in this cycle:

  • As CBOE/CME gradually accept crypto in-kind margin this year, the liquidity and availability of crypto assets as margin will significantly improve.

    • Once DTCC establishes a direct on-chain connection, the settlement layer will begin to penetrate onto the chain, allowing equity assets to obtain native on-chain settlement channels from the source. The truly interesting part then unfolds: tokenized stocks as collateral → perpetual contract exchanges accept them → institutions begin systematically performing basis farming. Onshore issuance, offshore distribution. The US exports finance not by exporting financial institutions themselves, but by exporting "access rights." The petrodollar system distributed dollars globally, spilling over inflationary pressures; stablecoins replicate this logic, wholesale US Treasury bonds, turning the world into new dollar holders without the need for banks or brokers. On-chain stocks are the next step in this logic. From unbanked to unbrokered, dollar assets will once again complete their global dumping. CEX recognized the opportunity and potential threats early on, and therefore chose to be among the first to expand. Ondo and xStocks focused on the issuance side—connecting with brokerages, custodying real stocks, and minting 1:1 tokenized stocks on multiple chains—but it turned out that issuance itself does not automatically create a market. The first wave of real demand came from traders who could not access the US brokerage system, and crypto native users who wanted to gain exposure to US stocks without relying on TradFi infrastructure. The issuers completed the most arduous compliance and custody work, but the funds flowed to the side that truly controlled trading attention and distribution capabilities. Offshore platforms directly embedded the products into their trading interfaces, naturally attracting a surge in trading volume. Ultimately, we see the vast majority of tokenized stock trading volume concentrated on BNB Chain, accounting for over 80%. If offshore spot trading unlocked retail demand, on-chain equity perp further attracted traffic from professional traders. These users are global trading participants who want to trade or hedge US stocks without being restricted by brokerage access, trading hours, or jurisdiction. Taking HIP-3 as an example, it provides professional traders with a trading interface that allows them to systematically perform basis trading, capture cross-market misalignments, and simultaneously cover stocks, crypto assets, and indices. Coupled with potential airdrop incentives, trading volume continues to reach new highs.

      The Golden Window for On-Chain Stock Perpetual Contracts

      Once a spot anchor exists, perpetual contracts almost always become the most efficient trading tool, for the same straightforward reasons:

      • 24/7 trading, unrestricted by market hours

      • Extremely high capital efficiency with cross-margining across all assets

data.title: From Roman silver coins to Bitcoin: Why are the scripts of capital "defection" always similar?
data.description:
Introduction

What is the essence of capital flight?

When markets tremble, currencies collapse, and trust crumbles, traditional narratives often attribute crises to "irrational panic."

However, this article, through historical depth and financial practice, reveals a more chilling truth: capital flight is not a systemic failure, but a highly rational economic warfare tactic.

The article analyzes classic cases, from the devaluation of Roman silver coins, the 1992 pound crisis, and the 1998 Russian debt collapse, to the Asian financial crisis, dissecting layer by layer how capital is weaponized.

Furthermore, this article extends its perspective to the era of blockchain and Web3. In the new battlefield of real-time clearing, transparent ledgers, and algorithmic defense, the speed and visibility of economic warfare have been elevated to the extreme.

Ultimately, a game-theoretic logic that has persisted for millennia becomes increasingly clear: The life or death of a monetary system does not depend on whether it is attacked, but on whether its promises have a credible defensive foundation. When trust is structurally eroded, the decision to withdraw capital is always made before it is officially recorded in the ledger. This article is not only a history of the evolution of financial conflicts, but also provides a penetrating analytical framework for understanding the vulnerabilities of current digital currencies, sovereign debt, and exchange rate systems. People often describe capital flight as a moment of market panic—irrational behavior driven by fear, rumors, or blind following. This interpretation seems reassuring because it suggests that crises are accidental and avoidable, driven by emotions rather than institutional flaws. But history offers a different answer. Capital flight is not a sign of irrationality, but rather a manifestation of economic strategy. When rational market participants reach a consensus that the commitments of a country or financial system are unsustainable, capital flight (capital withdrawing long before legal amendments, default declarations, or systemic collapse) occurs. In financial competition, the flow of capital is the key to victory. All monetary and financial systems are strategic constructs built upon enforceable commitments. Convertibility

  • Convertibility

  • Repayment Capacity

  • Stability

  • Rules-Based Governance

    • Defending these commitments requires reserves, credit backing, and most importantly, time.

    But questioning these commitments only requires shaking everyone's confidence, cooperating with all parties, and acting quickly enough. When the growth rate of debt exceeds the system's defense capacity, capital becomes a weapon—it tests the system's weaknesses, exerts market pressure, and then withdraws.

    People often use the seemingly neutral term "speculation" to describe this behavior, but in reality, it's a real attack on a financial system that's already on the verge of collapse. Throughout history, a small group of entities wielding economic power has consistently played a crucial role in these financial games. From ancient merchant networks and imperial reserve managers to modern liquidity providers and high-level speculators, they are usually the first to spot problems and act decisively. Their subtle actions, once they've seized the opportunity, can trigger a chain reaction throughout the entire financial system far exceeding expectations. Those crises that seem sudden in hindsight are actually just the final stage of a financial contest, the outcome quietly determined much earlier by the actions of various market participants, requiring no official announcement to reveal. Structural Game Theory: Capital Flight Simply put, the core of capital flight is: when the market is under immense pressure, people lose confidence in it. Regardless of whether it corresponds to silver coins, gold-based currencies, government-issued bonds, or digital tokens, confidence is the most crucial first line of defense for the financial system. Once a promise is made easily, but fulfilling it incurs exorbitant costs, those holding capital will clearly feel the urge to "quickly withdraw their money." At this point, those with money don't even consider whether the system will collapse; instead, they start wondering: if they continue to get involved, when will it become a disaster? This instant marks the beginning of the system's defenses crumbling, even if it appears perfectly fine on the surface. Capital flight is not simply market panic, but a rational action taken by all market participants. Those who withdraw capital first are not acting rashly, but making a rational choice in accordance with market rules.
